Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'd have these intense bursts where I'd study for hours straight, sometimes pulling all-nighters, and then I'd crash and not touch anything for days. With 11 online courses and everything else going on, I couldn't maintain any consistent schedule. I'd often find myself scrambling at the last minute, which I know isn't ideal, but my anxiety made it hard to approach coursework in a calm, planned way.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely open my laptop, and then suddenly I'd realize something was due and spend 31 hours cramming everything in. The irregular attendance—I know it's around 40%—reflects this perfectly. When my anxiety spiked, I'd either hyperfocus or completely shut down, there was rarely any middle ground.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd get a notification about a deadline or suddenly remember something was due, and that jolt of fear would get me online. Sometimes a peer from my support group would message me asking about an assignment, and that social nudge helped. The LMS became associated with stress for me, so I didn't exactly look forward to opening it.
Q4: There were brief periods, maybe a week or two, where logging in felt more routine—usually when I was in a better headspace or when the peer support intervention was really active. Having study buddies who expected to see me online helped create some accountability. But those routines never stuck for long because something would throw me off—a bad exam result, personal stress, or just exhaustion—and I'd fall out of the habit immediately.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and annotate them heavily. The videos were helpful too—I watched over 200 minutes worth—but I often had to re-read transcripts afterward to really absorb the content. The peer interaction features were surprisingly valuable because my support group kept me somewhat accountable.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—I think I only posted twice the entire course. The idea of putting my thoughts out there for everyone to see triggered my anxiety massively. I also delayed a lot of the optional practice quizzes because when motivation is as low as mine, anything not strictly required feels impossible to prioritize. I kept telling myself I'd get to them, but I rarely did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my exam performance or final grade. With my assignment completion hovering around 45%, I had to be strategic about where I put my limited energy. If something helped me understand a concept that I knew would be tested, I'd engage with it. The peer support sessions also felt worthwhile because they reduced my isolation.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, emotionally speaking. Required activities came with deadlines and consequences, which—as stressful as that was—actually forced me to act. Optional stuff just sat there making me feel guilty for not doing it. The pressure of required work was the only thing that could cut through my low motivation, even though it also fed my anxiety.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and lecture slides, then study them on my tablet without internet to avoid distractions. I have notebooks full of handwritten summaries and practice problems. I also used external YouTube videos and textbooks from the library. My exam score of 84 didn't come from LMS activity alone—it came from hours of invisible offline work.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be holed up in my room, working through downloaded materials and rewriting notes by hand, but my LMS would show zero activity. I'd print out past exams and work through them with a study partner in person. Sometimes I'd watch a lecture video once online, then review my notes from it multiple times over the following days without ever logging back in.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a PDF or video once, download or take detailed notes, and then that material would become part of my offline study routine for weeks. My LMS might show I viewed something for ten minutes, but I'd actually spend hours with that content in printed or downloaded form. It was more comfortable studying without the constant reminders of everything else I was behind on.
Q12: There were several points where engaging became nearly impossible. Mid-semester, my anxiety peaked and I could barely get out of bed some days. When I saw my assignment completion rate dropping, it created this vicious cycle—I'd feel worse about myself, which made it harder to engage, which made my completion rate drop further. Personal stuff outside of school compounded everything.
Q13: The workload from 11 courses was genuinely crushing, and my medium resources meant I couldn't just quit my part-time job to focus on studies. Fatigue was constant—I'd log into the LMS exhausted and just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid them entirely sometimes. Everything competed for my limited mental bandwidth.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, technically 'accessing' them, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other deadlines, feeling overwhelmed, or just mentally checked out. My high engagement level on paper doesn't capture how often I was physically present but mentally absent. Sometimes I'd watch videos without processing a single word.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd think about all the ways I could fail or how behind I already was, and paralysis would set in. Important tasks felt more threatening somehow, so I'd do smaller, less consequential things to feel productive while avoiding what really mattered. My 44.7% assignment completion rate is the evidence of this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS consistently. I'd log in frantically as due dates approached, trying to cram everything in. My login frequency of 40 times probably clusters heavily around deadline periods. Without that external pressure, I'd drift away from the platform entirely. Deadlines were both my enemy and my only reliable motivator.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost, but negative feedback could send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course entirely for days. With my low resilience, I took criticism very personally. Sometimes I'd delay checking my grades because I couldn't handle potentially bad news. The peer support helped buffer some of this by giving me people to process feedback with.
Q18: The course structure with its regular deadlines at least gave me external scaffolding, which I desperately needed given my low motivation. But the sheer volume of content across my 11 courses meant I couldn't keep up with the intended pace. I ended up creating my own chaotic structure based on what was most urgent rather than what the course designers intended.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier because I could study on my own terms offline. The peer interaction features helped too—those 31 peer interactions were lifelines during tough weeks. What made it harder was the overwhelming amount of content and the way everything felt high-stakes. The discussion requirements felt like public performances, which my anxiety couldn't handle.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat—high LMS activity but incomplete work. For me, it was the gap between intention and execution. I'd log in wanting to engage, spend time with materials, but then the actual task of producing and submitting work felt insurmountable. The anxiety of being evaluated on my output was different from just consuming content. Activity doesn't equal completion.
Q21: That also resonates with my experience in a way. My exam score was 84 despite my poor attendance and assignment completion because I studied intensively offline. Someone looking at my LMS data might think I was barely trying, but I was actually working hard—just not in visible ways. The system can't see the hours I spent with printed materials or in peer study sessions.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing around deadlines, and maybe consistency of engagement. But they miss the quality of attention, the offline work, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone watched a video while crying from stress or while genuinely engaged. They miss the invisible labor and the very real struggles that don't leave digital traces.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, even when it's hard. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's thinking about course concepts when you're not studying, wanting to understand rather than just pass. For me, engagement was always there internally, even when my anxiety and circumstances prevented it from showing up in my behavior or the LMS.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much the peer support intervention meant to me—those connections kept me from dropping out entirely. It can't show the panic attacks before logging in, or the relief when a study partner helped me understand something. It can't capture that I genuinely wanted to succeed but was fighting my own mind constantly. My merit grade came despite enormous invisible struggles, not because things were easy.
